title: Die Wichtelmänner
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 39. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The 39th tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 39) is really three separate little elf-tales printed together under one title. In the first, two naked mannikins secretly finish a poor shoemaker’s work each night until he and his wife reward them with tiny clothes, whereupon they dance away for good; in the second, a servant-girl stands godmother at an elf-christening in a hollow hill and comes home to find seven years have passed; in the third, a mother whose child the elves have swapped for a changeling exposes it by boiling water in eggshells until it laughs and betrays its ancient age. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Thüre, Noth, Rath, Muth, gieng and fieng, thun/that/thaten, wußte and ward.

sentences.t: Es war ein Schuster ohne seine Schuld so arm geworden, daß ihm endlich nichts mehr übrig blieb als Leder zu einem einzigen Paar Schuhe.
sentences.l: A shoemaker had, through no fault of his own, grown so poor that at last nothing remained left to him but leather for a single pair of shoes.
sentences.i: A shoemaker had, through no fault of his own, become so poor that at last nothing was left to him but leather enough for a single pair of shoes.
sentences.n: This is the first of three separate little elf-tales printed together under one title (the shoemaker, the godmother-maid, and the changeling). „ohne seine Schuld“ = ‘through no fault of his own.’ „daß“ is the 1857 spelling of dass, kept throughout this edition.

sentences.t: Nun schnitt er am Abend die Schuhe zu, die wollte er den nächsten Morgen in Arbeit nehmen; und weil er ein gutes Gewissen hatte, so legte er sich ruhig zu Bett, befahl sich dem lieben Gott und schlief ein.
sentences.l: Now in the evening he cut out the shoes, which he meant to take in hand the next morning; and because he had a good conscience, he laid himself calmly to bed, commended himself to the dear God, and fell asleep.
sentences.i: So in the evening he cut out the shoes, meaning to set to work on them the next morning; and because he had a good conscience, he lay down calmly, commended himself to the dear Lord, and fell asleep.
sentences.n: „schnitt ... zu“ is the separable verb zuschneiden, ‘to cut out (to shape)’ — the prefix zu jumps to the end. „befahl sich dem lieben Gott“: sich befehlen + dative, ‘to commend oneself to God.’ „schlief ein“ (einschlafen, ‘to fall asleep’) is also separable.

sentences.t: Morgens, nachdem er sein Gebet verrichtet hatte und sich zur Arbeit niedersetzen wollte, so standen die beiden Schuhe ganz fertig auf seinem Tisch.
sentences.l: In the morning, after he had performed his prayer and wanted to sit down to work, the two shoes stood quite finished on his table.
sentences.i: In the morning, after he had said his prayers and was about to sit down to work, there stood the two shoes, quite finished, on his table.
sentences.n: „sein Gebet verrichten“ = to say / perform one’s prayer. „niedersetzen“ (sich niedersetzen, ‘to sit down’) is separable. The resumptive „so“ after a long clause is a Grimm habit — leave it untranslated.

sentences.t: Er verwunderte sich und wußte nicht was er dazu sagen sollte.
sentences.l: He marvelled and did not know what he should say to it.
sentences.i: He was amazed and did not know what to make of it.
sentences.n: „verwunderte sich“ — the reflexive sich verwundern, ‘to be astonished.’ „wußte“ is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Er nahm die Schuhe in die Hand um sie näher zu betrachten: sie waren so sauber gearbeitet, daß kein Stich daran falsch war, gerade als wenn es ein Meisterstück sein sollte.
sentences.l: He took the shoes in his hand to look at them more closely: they were so cleanly worked that not a stitch in them was wrong, just as if it were to be a masterpiece.
sentences.i: He took the shoes in his hand to examine them more closely: they were so neatly made that not a single stitch was amiss, exactly as though it were meant to be a masterpiece.
sentences.n: „sauber gearbeitet“ = neatly / cleanly worked. „als wenn“ = ‘as if’ (modern als ob), taking the subjunctive „sollte.“

sentences.t: Bald darauf trat auch schon ein Käufer ein, und weil ihm die Schuhe so gut gefielen, so bezahlte er mehr als gewöhnlich dafür, und der Schuster konnte von dem Geld Leder zu zwei Paar Schuhen erhandeln.
sentences.l: Soon afterward a buyer already stepped in, and because the shoes pleased him so well, he paid more than usual for them, and out of the money the shoemaker could purchase leather for two pairs of shoes.
sentences.i: Soon afterward a customer came in, and because the shoes pleased him so well, he paid more than usual for them, and out of the money the shoemaker was able to buy leather for two pairs of shoes.
sentences.n: „trat ... ein“ (eintreten, ‘to enter’) is separable. „ihm ... gefielen“ — gefallen takes the dative person: the shoes were pleasing to him. „erhandeln“ = to get by bargaining, buy.

sentences.t: Er schnitt sie Abends zu und wollte den nächsten Morgen mit frischem Muth an die Arbeit gehen, aber er brauchte es nicht, denn als er aufstand waren sie schon fertig, und es blieben auch nicht die Käufer aus, die ihm so viel Geld gaben daß er Leder zu vier Paar Schuhen einkaufen konnte.
sentences.l: He cut them out in the evening and meant the next morning to go to work with fresh spirit, but he did not need to, for when he got up they were already finished, and neither did the buyers stay away, who gave him so much money that he could buy leather for four pairs of shoes.
sentences.i: He cut them out in the evening and meant to set to work the next morning with fresh heart, but he had no need to, for when he rose they were already finished; nor did the customers fail to come, and they gave him so much money that he could buy leather for four pairs of shoes.
sentences.n: „mit frischem Muth“ = with fresh spirit / courage (Muth is the old spelling of Mut). „blieben ... aus“ (ausbleiben, ‘to stay away, fail to appear’) is separable, as is „einkaufen.“

sentences.t: Er fand früh Morgens auch die vier Paar fertig; und so giengs immer fort, was er Abends zuschnitt, das war am Morgen verarbeitet, also daß er bald wieder sein ehrliches Auskommen hatte und endlich ein wohlhabender Mann ward.
sentences.l: Early in the morning he found the four pairs finished too; and so it went on ever, whatever he cut out in the evening was worked up by morning, so that he soon again had his honest livelihood and at last became a well-to-do man.
sentences.i: Early in the morning he found the four pairs finished too; and so it went on: whatever he cut out in the evening was made up by morning, so that he soon had an honest livelihood again and at last became a prosperous man.
sentences.n: „giengs“ = gieng es (‘it went’); gieng is the 1857 spelling of ging. „fort“ with gehen here = to go on, continue. „ward“ is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became’), everywhere in Grimm.

sentences.t: Nun geschah es eines Abends nicht lange vor Weihnachten, als der Mann wieder zugeschnitten hatte, daß er vor Schlafengehen zu seiner Frau sprach „wie wärs wenn wir diese Nacht aufblieben um zu sehen wer uns solche hilfreiche Hand leistet?“
sentences.l: Now it happened one evening not long before Christmas, when the man had cut out again, that before going to bed he said to his wife, “how would it be if we stayed up this night to see who lends us such a helpful hand?”
sentences.i: Now it came to pass one evening not long before Christmas, when the man had cut out his leather again, that before going to bed he said to his wife, “How would it be if we stayed up tonight to see who it is that lends us such a helping hand?”
sentences.n: „wie wärs“ = wie wäre es, ‘how would it be’ (a colloquial contraction). „aufblieben“ is subjunctive of aufbleiben (‘to stay up’). „Hand leisten“ — jemandem hilfreiche Hand leisten, to lend someone a helping hand.

sentences.t: Die Frau wars zufrieden und steckte ein Licht an; darauf verbargen sie sich in den Stubenecken, hinter den Kleidern, die da aufgehängt waren und gaben acht.
sentences.l: The wife was content with it and lit a light; thereupon they hid themselves in the corners of the room, behind the clothes that were hung up there, and paid heed.
sentences.i: The wife was content, and lit a candle; then they hid themselves in the corners of the room, behind the clothes that hung there, and kept watch.
sentences.n: „wars zufrieden“ = war es zufrieden, ‘was content with it.’ „steckte ... an“ (anstecken, ‘to light’) and the participle „aufgehängt“ (aufhängen, ‘to hang up’) are separable. „acht geben“ = to pay attention, keep watch.

sentences.t: Als es Mitternacht war, da kamen zwei kleine niedliche nackte Männlein, setzten sich vor des Schusters Tisch, nahmen alle zugeschnittene Arbeit zu sich und fiengen an mit ihren Fingerlein so behend und schnell zu stechen, zu nähen, zu klopfen, daß der Schuster vor Verwunderung die Augen nicht abwenden konnte.
sentences.l: When it was midnight, there came two little, dainty, naked mannikins, sat down before the shoemaker’s table, took all the cut-out work to themselves, and began with their little fingers to prick, to sew, to tap so nimbly and fast that the shoemaker could not turn his eyes away for amazement.
sentences.i: When it was midnight, there came two little, dainty, naked mannikins; they sat down at the shoemaker’s table, took up all the cut-out work, and began with their tiny fingers to stitch and sew and tap so nimbly and quickly that the shoemaker could not turn his eyes away for wonder.
sentences.n: „Männlein“ and „Fingerlein“ are diminutives (‑lein): little men, little fingers. „fiengen an“ (anfangen, ‘to begin’) is separable; fiengen is the 1857 spelling. „des Schusters Tisch“ is a genitive placed before its noun, ‘the shoemaker’s table.’

sentences.t: Sie ließen nicht nach, bis alles zu Ende gebracht war und fertig auf dem Tische stand, dann sprangen sie schnell fort.
sentences.l: They did not let up until all was brought to an end and stood finished on the table, then they sprang quickly away.
sentences.i: They did not stop until everything was brought to an end and stood finished on the table, and then they leapt swiftly away.
sentences.n: „ließen ... nach“ (nachlassen, ‘to let up, slacken’) is separable. „Tische“ has the old dative ‑e. „sprangen ... fort“ (fortspringen, ‘to spring away’) is separable.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen sprach die Frau „die kleinen Männer haben uns reich gemacht, wir müßten uns doch dankbar dafür bezeigen.
sentences.l: The next morning the wife spoke, “the little men have made us rich; we really ought to show ourselves grateful for it.
sentences.i: The next morning the wife said, “The little men have made us rich; we really ought to show them our gratitude for it.
sentences.n: „andern“ is the old spelling of anderen. „müßten“ is the subjunctive of müssen (‘we ought to,’ softened by „doch“). „sich dankbar bezeigen“ = to show oneself grateful.

sentences.t: Sie laufen so herum, haben nichts am Leib und müssen frieren.
sentences.l: They run about so, have nothing on their body, and must freeze.
sentences.i: They run about like that with nothing on their bodies, and must be freezing.
sentences.n: „am Leib“ = on the body; „nichts am Leib haben“ = to have nothing on, be naked. „frieren“ = to freeze, be cold.

sentences.t: Weißt du was? ich will Hemdlein, Rock, Wams und Höslein für sie nähen, auch jedem ein Paar Strümpfe stricken; mach du jedem ein Paar Schühlein dazu.“
sentences.l: Do you know what? I will sew little shirts, a coat, a doublet, and little breeches for them, also knit each of them a pair of stockings; you make each a pair of little shoes to go with it.”
sentences.i: Do you know what? I will sew little shirts, coats, doublets and little breeches for them, and knit each a pair of stockings; and you make each a pair of little shoes as well.”
sentences.n: Diminutives again: „Hemdlein“ (little shirt), „Höslein“ (little breeches), „Schühlein“ (little shoes). „Wams“ = a doublet (old close-fitting jacket). „mach du“ — the du is added for contrast, ‘you make.’

sentences.t: Der Mann sprach „das bin ich wohl zufrieden,“ und Abends, wie sie alles fertig hatten, legten sie die Geschenke statt der zugeschnittenen Arbeit zusammen auf den Tisch und versteckten sich dann, um mit anzusehen wie sich die Männlein dazu anstellen würden.
sentences.l: The man spoke, “with that I am well content,” and in the evening, when they had everything ready, they laid the gifts together on the table instead of the cut-out work and then hid themselves, in order to look on at how the mannikins would set about it.
sentences.i: The man said, “I am quite content with that,” and in the evening, when they had everything ready, they laid the gifts on the table instead of the cut-out work, and then hid themselves to watch how the mannikins would go about it.
sentences.n: „das bin ich wohl zufrieden“ = ‘I am quite content with that’ (zufrieden with an accusative object is an older usage). „mit anzusehen“ — zusehen / ansehen with mit, ‘to look on.’ „sich anstellen“ = to set about, behave.

sentences.t: Um Mitternacht kamen sie herangesprungen und wollten sich gleich an die Arbeit machen, als sie aber kein zugeschnittenes Leder, sondern die niedlichen Kleidungsstücke fanden, verwunderten sie sich erst, dann aber bezeigten sie eine gewaltige Freude.
sentences.l: At midnight they came springing up and wanted to set to work at once, but when they found no cut-out leather, but rather the dainty garments, they marvelled at first, but then showed a mighty joy.
sentences.i: At midnight they came leaping up and wanted to set to work at once, but when they found no cut-out leather, but instead the dainty garments, at first they were astonished, and then showed a tremendous delight.
sentences.n: „herangesprungen“ (heranspringen, ‘to come leaping up’) is separable. „sich an die Arbeit machen“ = to set to work. „nicht ... sondern“ = ‘not ... but rather.’ „bezeigten ... Freude“ = displayed joy.

sentences.t: Mit der größten Geschwindigkeit zogen sie sich an, strichen die schönen Kleider am Leib und sangen
sentences.l: With the greatest speed they dressed themselves, stroked the fine clothes smooth on their bodies, and sang
sentences.i: With the greatest speed they dressed themselves, smoothed the fine clothes down on their bodies, and sang:
sentences.n: „zogen sich an“ (sich anziehen, ‘to get dressed’) is separable. „strichen ... am Leib“ — streichen, here to stroke / smooth the clothes down against the body.

sentences.t:
„sind wir nicht Knaben glatt und fein?
was sollen wir länger Schuster sein!“
sentences.l:
“are we not lads smooth and fine?
why should we be cobblers any longer!”
sentences.i:
“Are we not lads so sleek and fine?
Why should we cobblers be any longer!”
sentences.n: A little rhyming song (fein / sein). „glatt und fein“ = sleek and fine, nicely turned out. „was sollen wir länger Schuster sein“ — here „was“ carries the sense ‘why’: why should we go on being cobblers.

sentences.t: Dann hüpften und tanzten sie, und sprangen über Stühle und Bänke.
sentences.l: Then they hopped and danced, and sprang over chairs and benches.

sentences.t: Endlich tanzten sie zur Thüre hinaus.
sentences.l: At last they danced out at the door.
sentences.i: At last they danced out of the door.
sentences.n: „zur Thüre hinaus“ = out through the door. „Thüre“ is the 1857 spelling of Tür, with the old ‑th‑.

sentences.t: Von nun an kamen sie nicht wieder, dem Schuster aber gieng es wohl so lang er lebte, und es glückte ihm alles was er unternahm.
sentences.l: From now on they did not come again, but the shoemaker fared well as long as he lived, and everything he undertook succeeded for him.
sentences.i: From then on they never came again; but the shoemaker fared well as long as he lived, and everything he undertook prospered for him.
sentences.n: „dem Schuster gieng es wohl“ — the impersonal dative idiom es geht jemandem wohl, ‘to fare well.’ „es glückte ihm“ = it succeeded for him (glücken + dative).

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein armes Dienstmädchen, das war fleißig und reinlich, kehrte alle Tage das Haus und schüttete das Kehricht auf einen großen Haufen vor die Thüre.
sentences.l: There was once a poor servant-maid who was industrious and clean; she swept the house every day and poured the sweepings onto a great heap before the door.
sentences.i: Once there was a poor servant-girl who was hard-working and clean; she swept the house every day and threw the sweepings onto a great heap outside the door.
sentences.n: This begins the second of the three tales. „kehren“ = to sweep; „Kehricht“ = the sweepings, refuse. „das war fleißig“ — das refers to the neuter Dienstmädchen, so grammatically ‘it,’ but read as ‘she.’

sentences.t: Eines Morgens, als es eben wieder an die Arbeit gehen wollte, fand es einen Brief darauf, und weil es nicht lesen konnte, so stellte es den Besen in die Ecke und brachte den Brief seiner Herrschaft, und da war es eine Einladung von den Wichtelmännern, die baten das Mädchen ihnen ein Kind aus der Taufe zu heben.
sentences.l: One morning, as it was just about to go to work again, it found a letter on it, and because it could not read, it set the broom in the corner and brought the letter to its master and mistress; and it was an invitation from the elves, who asked the girl to lift a child for them out of the font.
sentences.i: One morning, as she was just about to set to work again, she found a letter on it, and because she could not read, she stood the broom in the corner and took the letter to her employers; and it was an invitation from the elves, who asked the girl to be godmother to a child of theirs.
sentences.n: The neuter „es“ throughout is das Dienstmädchen / Mädchen — read ‘she.’ „Herrschaft“ = the master and mistress, the employers. „ein Kind aus der Taufe heben“ is a set phrase, ‘to hold a child at the font,’ i.e. to stand as its godparent.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen wußte nicht was es thun sollte, endlich auf vieles Zureden, und weil sie ihm sagten so etwas dürfte man nicht abschlagen, so willigte es ein.
sentences.l: The girl did not know what it should do; at last, on much urging, and because they told it that such a thing one might not refuse, it consented.
sentences.i: The girl did not know what to do; at last, after much urging, and because they told her that one must not refuse such a thing, she consented.
sentences.n: „thun“ is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’). „auf vieles Zureden“ = after much persuasion. „abschlagen“ = to refuse, deny. „willigte ... ein“ (einwilligen, ‘to consent’) is separable.

sentences.t: Da kamen drei Wichtelmänner und führten es in einen hohlen Berg, wo die Kleinen lebten.
sentences.l: Then three elves came and led it into a hollow hill, where the little ones lived.
sentences.i: Then three elves came and led her into a hollow hill, where the little folk lived.
sentences.n: „hohlen“ (hohl, ‘hollow’). „die Kleinen“ = the little ones, the elves (an adjective used as a noun).

sentences.t: Es war da alles klein, aber so zierlich und prächtig daß es nicht zu sagen ist.
sentences.l: Everything there was small, but so dainty and splendid that it cannot be told.
sentences.i: Everything there was small, yet so dainty and magnificent that it cannot be described.
sentences.n: „es ist nicht zu sagen“ = it cannot be told (zu + infinitive with sein expresses possibility).

sentences.t: Die Kindbetterin lag in einem Bett von schwarzem Ebenholz mit Knöpfen von Perlen, die Decken waren mit Gold gestickt, die Wiege war von Elfenbein die Badwanne von Gold.
sentences.l: The woman in childbed lay in a bed of black ebony with knobs of pearl; the covers were embroidered with gold, the cradle was of ivory, the bathtub of gold.
sentences.i: The new mother lay in a bed of black ebony with knobs of pearl; the coverlets were embroidered with gold, the cradle was of ivory and the bathtub of gold.
sentences.n: „Kindbetterin“ = a woman lying in childbed, a new mother. „Knöpfen von Perlen“ = knobs (buttons) of pearl. „mit Gold gestickt“ = embroidered with gold (sticken, ‘to embroider’).

sentences.t: Das Mädchen stand nun Gevatter und wollte dann wieder nach Haus gehen, die Wichtelmännlein baten es aber inständig drei Tage bei ihnen zu bleiben.
sentences.l: The girl now stood godmother and then wanted to go home again, but the little elves earnestly begged it to stay three days with them.
sentences.i: The girl now stood godmother, and then wanted to go home again; but the little elves earnestly begged her to stay three days with them.
sentences.n: „Gevatter stehen“ = to stand as godparent (Gevatter, a sponsor at baptism). „inständig“ = urgently, earnestly. „Wichtelmännlein“ is the diminutive, ‘little elves.’

sentences.t: Es blieb also und verlebte die Zeit in Lust und Freude, und die Kleinen thaten ihm alles zu Liebe.
sentences.l: So it stayed and passed the time in delight and joy, and the little ones did it every kindness.
sentences.i: So she stayed and spent the time in delight and joy, and the little folk did her every kindness.
sentences.n: „verlebte“ (verleben, ‘to spend, pass’ time). „thaten“ is the 1857 spelling of taten (‘did’; thun = tun). „jemandem etwas zu Liebe thun“ = to do something to please someone.

sentences.t: Endlich wollte es sich auf den Rückweg machen, da steckten sie ihm die Taschen erst ganz voll Gold und führten es hernach wieder zum Berge heraus.
sentences.l: At last it wanted to set out on the way back; then they first stuffed its pockets quite full of gold and afterward led it out of the hill again.
sentences.i: At last she wanted to set out on the way back, whereupon they first filled her pockets quite full of gold, and then led her out of the hill again.
sentences.n: „sich auf den Rückweg machen“ = to set off on the way back. „steckten ... voll“ = to stuff full. „hernach“ = afterward. „zum Berge heraus“ = out of the hill (Berge has the old dative ‑e).

sentences.t: Als es nach Haus kam, wollte es seine Arbeit beginnen, nahm den Besen in die Hand, der noch in der Ecke stand und fieng an zu kehren.
sentences.l: When it came home, it wanted to begin its work, took the broom in its hand, which still stood in the corner, and began to sweep.
sentences.i: When she came home, she wanted to begin her work, took up the broom, which still stood in the corner, and began to sweep.
sentences.n: „fieng an“ (anfangen, ‘to begin’) is separable; fieng is the 1857 spelling of fing. The broom stands exactly where she left it — a quiet hint that for her no time has passed.

sentences.t: Da kamen fremde Leute aus dem Haus, die fragten wer es wäre und was es da zu thun hätte.
sentences.l: Then strange people came out of the house, who asked who it was and what it had to do there.
sentences.i: Then strangers came out of the house and asked who she was and what she had to do there.
sentences.n: „wäre“ and „hätte“ are subjunctive II of the reported question. „fremde Leute“ = strangers, unfamiliar people.

sentences.t: Da war es nicht drei Tage, wie es gemeint hatte, sondern sieben Jahre bei den kleinen Männern im Berge gewesen, und seine vorige Herrschaft war in der Zeit gestorben.
sentences.l: Then it had not been three days, as it had thought, but seven years with the little men in the hill, and its former master and mistress had died in that time.
sentences.i: It turned out that she had been with the little men in the hill not three days, as she had thought, but seven years, and her former employers had died in the meantime.
sentences.n: The folklore motif: time in the fairy hill runs differently — three days there are seven years in the human world. „gemeint hatte“ (meinen, ‘to think, suppose’). „vorige Herrschaft“ = former employers.

sentences.t: Einer Mutter war ihr Kind von den Wichtelmännern aus der Wiege geholt, und ein Wechselbalg mit dickem Kopf und starren Augen hineingelegt, der nichts als essen und trinken wollte.
sentences.l: From a mother her child had been fetched out of the cradle by the elves, and a changeling with a thick head and staring eyes laid in it instead, which wanted nothing but to eat and drink.
sentences.i: A mother’s child had been taken out of the cradle by the elves, and a changeling with a big head and staring eyes laid in its place, which wanted nothing but to eat and drink.
sentences.n: This begins the third tale — the changeling story. „Einer Mutter war ihr Kind ... geholt“ uses a dative of the possessor: literally ‘from a mother her child had been fetched.’ „Wechselbalg“ = a changeling, the elf-child left in place of a stolen human baby. „hineingelegt“ (hineinlegen, ‘to lay inside’) is separable.

sentences.t: In ihrer Noth gieng sie zu ihrer Nachbarin und fragte sie um Rath.
sentences.l: In her distress she went to her neighbour and asked her for advice.
sentences.n: „Noth“ (1857 spelling of Not, ‘distress, need’) and „Rath“ (1857 spelling of Rat, ‘advice’) both keep the old ‑th‑. „jemanden um Rath fragen“ = to ask someone for advice.

sentences.t: Die Nachbarin sagte sie sollte den Wechselbalg in die Küche tragen, auf den Herd setzen, Feuer anmachen und in zwei Eierschalen Wasser kochen: das bringe den Wechselbalg zum Lachen, und wenn er lache, dann sei es aus mit ihm.
sentences.l: The neighbour said she should carry the changeling into the kitchen, set it on the hearth, make a fire, and boil water in two eggshells: that would bring the changeling to laughing, and when it laughed, then it would be over with it.
sentences.i: The neighbour said she should carry the changeling into the kitchen, set it on the hearth, make a fire, and boil water in two eggshells: that would make the changeling laugh, and once it laughed, it would be all over with it.
sentences.n: „anmachen“ (Feuer anmachen, ‘to light a fire’) is separable. „bringe“, „lache“ and „sei“ are subjunctive I — the neighbour’s advice in reported speech. „zum Lachen bringen“ = to make (someone) laugh. „es ist aus mit ihm“ = ‘it is over with it,’ its spell is broken.

sentences.t: Die Frau that alles wie die Nachbarin gesagt hatte.
sentences.l: The woman did everything as the neighbour had said.
sentences.n: „that“ is the 1857 spelling of tat (‘did’; thun = modern tun).

sentences.t: Wie sie die Eierschalen mit Wasser über das Feuer setzte, sprach der Klotzkopf
sentences.l: As she set the eggshells with water over the fire, the blockhead spoke
sentences.i: As she set the water-filled eggshells over the fire, the blockhead said:
sentences.n: „Klotzkopf“ = ‘blockhead,’ a mocking name for the dull, big-headed changeling. „wie“ here = ‘as, when.’

sentences.t:
„nun bin ich so alt
wie der Westerwald,
und hab nicht gesehen daß jemand in Schalen kocht.“
sentences.l:
“now I am as old
as the Westerwald,
and have not seen that anyone cooks in shells.”
sentences.i:
“Now I am as old
as the Westerwald itself,
and never have I seen a soul cook in eggshells.”
sentences.n: The trick: by exclaiming that in all its long life it has never seen anything so absurd, the changeling betrays its true, ancient age — and by speaking at all, breaks the spell. „der Westerwald“ is a low mountain range in western Germany, proverbially old. „hab“ drops the final ‑e (habe). The rhyme is alt / Westerwald.

sentences.t: Und fieng an darüber zu lachen.
sentences.l: And began to laugh at it.
sentences.i: And it began to laugh at it.
sentences.n: „fieng an“ (anfangen) once more, the separable ‘began.’ „darüber“ = at / about it.

sentences.t: Indem er lachte kam auf einmal eine Menge von Wichtelmännerchen, die brachten das rechte Kind, setzten es auf den Herd und nahmen den Wechselbalg wieder mit fort.
sentences.l: While it laughed, all at once a crowd of little elves came, who brought the right child, set it on the hearth, and took the changeling away with them again.
sentences.i: As it laughed, all at once a crowd of little elves appeared; they brought the real child, set it on the hearth, and carried the changeling off with them again.
sentences.n: „indem“ here = ‘while, as.’ „Wichtelmännerchen“ is a double diminutive, ‘little elf-folk.’ „das rechte Kind“ = the real, true child. „nahmen ... mit fort“ (mitnehmen + fort) = took away with them.
